title: Amazon stock doubles down on grocery efforts, challenging Walmart
content:
Investing.com - Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) is intensifying its focus on online grocery delivery and expanding its Whole Foods store base, according to a company blog post that has pressured grocery-related stocks.

The e-commerce giant acknowledged it hasn’t yet developed a distinctive customer experience in physical grocery with the economic model needed for large-scale expansion, according to Mizuho’s research note titled "AMZN Grocery with Another Direct Shot at WMT."

Walmart (NYSE:WMT) shares experienced the largest decline among grocery retailers following the announcement, with Mizuho noting that Walmart’s 4,000+ store network remains its most differentiating factor in the grocery competition unless Amazon makes a significant grocery-focused acquisition.

Mizuho analysts observed that Amazon’s grocery efforts appear more focused on urban markets versus rural or suburban areas, leveraging the company’s strength in densified markets.

In other recent news, Costco Wholesale Corporation announced a quarterly cash dividend of $1.30 per share, payable on February 13, 2026, to shareholders of record by January 30, 2026. Analysts have also weighed in on Costco’s performance, with Bernstein maintaining an Outperform rating, citing the company as undervalued despite its stock’s recovery. UBS continues to support a Buy rating, highlighting Costco’s strong December comparable sales performance, which saw a 6.3% increase in core U.S. sales.

Jefferies also reiterated a Buy rating, noting an 8.5% growth in net sales for December and favorable trends in ticket size and customer traffic. Meanwhile, DA Davidson maintained a Neutral rating, observing a 7.0% total comparable sales growth in December. These developments reflect a generally positive outlook from analysts, with several firms maintaining high price targets for Costco.

title: Amazon to convert select physical stores into Whole Foods, expand same-day delivery
content:
Jan 27 (Reuters) - Amazon said on Tuesday it would shutter its Amazon Go and Amazon Fresh physical stores and convert a number of those locations into Whole Foods Market, the latest push in the company’s efforts to beef up its grocery business.

The move is part of Amazon’s plan to open more than 100 new Whole Foods stores over the next few years as it bets on the upmarket grocery brand to help it compete better with retailers such as Walmart and Kroger, as well as delivery services like Instacart.

Amazon said it would also expand its same-day delivery service to more U.S. cities this year to cash in on the surging demand for household essentials on its platform, where more than 150 million customers shop for groceries annually.

The Amazon Fresh banner - known for allowing customers to skip the checkout line when picking up groceries at brick-and-mortar stores - will continue to operate online in available areas, the company said.

"While we’ve seen encouraging signals in our Amazon-branded physical grocery stores, we haven’t yet created a truly distinctive customer experience with the right economic model needed for large-scale expansion," Amazon said in a statement.

The closures were meant to "prioritize investment in growth areas", the tech giant said.

Outside of Fresh stores, Amazon has also tried multiple different brick-and-mortar concepts including bookstores and clothing, as well as stores stocking only the highest-rated items on its retail website.

The cashier-less convenience store brand Amazon Go, rolled out in 2018, offered customers prepared foods and snacks along with groceries.

The pivot follows a similar move by Amazon in the UK, where it closed all of its Amazon Fresh stores and converted some of them into Whole Foods locations.

Amazon has been working to integrate Whole Foods more closely within its operations. Last year, it extended its corporate staff programs, including pay structure and benefits, to the Whole Foods corporate workforce.

The company has invested heavily in speeding up delivery timelines to cater to customer demand for perishables and now offers online grocery delivery in over 5,000 cities and towns in the United States. It recently started testing 30-minute deliveries in Seattle and Philadelphia.

Amazon did not share the number of employees that would be impacted by the store closures. However, it said it was working to help associates find other roles elsewhere within Amazon.

title: Stifel raises Amazon.com stock price target to $300 on advertising strength
content:
Investing.com - Stifel raised its price target on Amazon.com (NASDAQ:AMZN) to $300.00 from $295.00 on Tuesday, while maintaining a Buy rating on the e-commerce and cloud computing giant. The new target represents a 24% upside from Amazon’s current trading price of $241.10.

Stifel cited positive indicators in Amazon’s advertising business as a key factor behind the revised outlook, along with what it described as a "healthy" fourth-quarter e-commerce environment and strong holiday shopping season.

The firm also expressed confidence in Amazon Web Services (AWS), the company’s cloud computing division, noting that its growth trajectory "looks more than reasonable."

While Stifel acknowledged concerns about consumer spending weakness, it believes Amazon has sufficient strengths to offset these challenges, predicting the stock will outperform in 2026 after underperforming last year.

In other recent news, Amazon is set to report its fourth-quarter earnings, with BofA Securities projecting revenue of $213 billion and operating income of $26 billion, surpassing the Street consensus. The company has also secured a significant $581 million contract from the Pentagon to support the U.S. Air Force’s Cloud One Program, a deal set to last until December 2028. In analyst updates, BofA Securities has lowered its price target for Amazon to $286 while maintaining a Buy rating, reflecting expectations ahead of the earnings report. Evercore ISI has reiterated an Outperform rating with a $335 price target, noting improvements in Amazon’s cost structure. Additionally, Bernstein SocGen Group continues to view Amazon favorably, maintaining an Outperform rating and a $300 price target, highlighting potential growth in 2026.

This article was generated with the support of AI and reviewed by an editor. For more information see our T&C.

title: Amazon grocery push sinks Kroger, Instacart, delivery stocks
content:
Investing.com -- Kroger (NYSE:KR) stock fell 1.6% Tuesday, while Instacart (NASDAQ:CART) tumbled 5% after Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) announced plans to double down on its online grocery delivery services and expand Whole Foods Market locations.

The e-commerce giant’s aggressive grocery strategy also dragged down other food delivery stocks, with Uber (NYSE:UBER) dropping 2.5%, DoorDash (NYSE:DASH) falling 1.8%, and Walmart (NYSE:WMT) slipping 1%.

Amazon revealed plans to expand its Same-Day Delivery service for fresh groceries to more communities in 2026, building on what it described as strong customer feedback. The company said perishable grocery sales through this service have grown 40x since January 2025.

Additionally, Amazon announced it will open more than 100 new Whole Foods Market stores over the next few years while closing its Amazon Go and Amazon Fresh physical stores. Some of these locations will be converted to Whole Foods stores.

The Seattle-based tech giant highlighted that it has become one of the top three grocers in the U.S., with over $150 billion in gross sales and more than 150 million customers shopping for groceries each year. Everyday household essentials now represent one out of every three units sold on Amazon.com.

Amazon is also testing Amazon Now, an ultra-fast delivery option that brings thousands of essential items, including fresh food, to customers’ homes in about 30 minutes or less, further intensifying competition in the grocery delivery space.

The company’s expansion of Whole Foods Market Daily Shop, a smaller store format offering grab-and-go meals and everyday essentials, adds another dimension to its growing grocery presence that threatens traditional supermarket chains and delivery platforms alike.

title: Microsoft stock undervalued despite Azure growth, says DA Davidson
content:
Investing.com - Microsoft’s Azure cloud service is growing faster than competitors Google Cloud Platform and Amazon Web Services, according to DA Davidson analyst Gil Luria in a recent "Mega (Cap) Preview" report.

Despite this performance advantage, Luria notes that Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) stock is trading at the lower end of its historical multiple range, while Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOGL) trades at the high end of its range, suggesting a potential valuation disconnect.

For Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META), DA Davidson expects revenue growth to continue above 20%, but cautions that investor reaction will depend more on updated operating expense and capital expenditure guidance than on top-line performance.

Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) is projected to deliver a rare double-digit growth quarter driven by the iPhone upgrade cycle, though the firm warns this could set up difficult year-over-year comparisons in the future without compelling new products like an enhanced Siri or folding iPhone.

Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) is expected to report strong results from both consumer business and cloud computing, with Luria specifically citing "a good quarter on both sides" driven by a strong consumer Q4 and increasing compute demand from AI company Anthropic.

In other recent news, Alphabet has seen several updates that may interest investors. KeyBanc has raised its price target for Alphabet to $360 from $330, maintaining an Overweight rating. The firm emphasized the role of artificial intelligence trends, such as compute capabilities and proprietary data, in bolstering Alphabet’s performance. Similarly, Stifel increased its price target for Alphabet to $346 from $333, citing strong expectations for the company’s advertising results in the fourth quarter, particularly for Search and YouTube. title: Internet Stocks 2026: Wolfe Research Reveals its Top Picks for the Year
content:
Investing.com -- The internet sector, while not expected to match the extraordinary gains of the past three years, still offers promising investment opportunities in 2026.

Wolfe Research highlights several stocks poised for growth driven by AI developments, product catalysts, and successful capital allocation strategies.

After a 35% market cap weighted increase in 2025 (compared to 43% in 2024 and 94% in 2023), the sector continues to outperform the broader market.

AI-related capital expenditure remains a focal point for investors, with early winners likely to maintain their advantage. Here are Wolfe Research’s top internet stock picks for 2026:

Wolfe Research expects 2026 to be "Amazon’s year," following META in 2024 and GOOGL in 2025. Their bullish outlook stems from projected AWS growth acceleration to at least mid-20% in 2026 and 2027, with estimates exceeding Street consensus by $6 billion and $13 billion respectively.

Anthropic is expected to contribute approximately $8 billion in 2026, while OpenAI could add $4.5-$5 billion. Retail revenue growth should maintain high single digits, driven by faster delivery capabilities, market consolidation, and grocery expansion.

Operating margins are projected to exceed Street estimates by 28 basis points, supported by automation, advertising growth, and productivity gains. At 23x projected 2027 earnings, valuation remains reasonable compared to peers.

Amazon Web Services recently secured a $581 million cloud contract from the Pentagon to support the U.S. Air Force’s Cloud One Program. Separately, BofA Securities lowered its price target on the company, while firms including Bernstein and Evercore ISI reiterated Outperform ratings.

Meta Platforms

As Wolfe’s second top pick, META’s path to 20% ad revenue growth in 2026 is supported by monetization of Threads and WhatsApp (low to mid-single-digit billions uplift), Click-to-message features (approximately $4 billion incremental), and GEM + Andromeda improvements.

Operating and capital expenditure guidance should provide clarity on frontier AI model development. Sentiment around AI capabilities is expected to improve with competitive model releases. At 19x projected 2027 earnings versus GOOGL’s 24x, META offers attractive relative valuation.

Meta Platforms is investing up to $6 billion in Corning’s fiber-optic cable technology through 2030 to support its expanding AI data center needs. The company also received a rating upgrade to Buy from Rothschild Redburn, while KeyBanc lowered its price target.

Wolfe’s third large-cap selection is projected to deliver low single-digit percentage upside to gross order value versus consensus, driven by durable consumer demand, Deliveroo outperformance, and expanding grocery penetration.

FY2027 EBITDA could exceed estimates by 3%, benefiting from automation ($150 million), improved international unit economics, and increased order volume. The stock trades at a reasonable 0.6x FY2027 EBITDA multiple on a growth-adjusted basis.

DoorDash has formed a partnership with athletic retailer Hibbett to offer nationwide on-demand delivery. The company also appointed former Tesla robotics executive Milan Kovac to its Board of Directors.

As Wolfe’s top small/mid-cap pick, CHWY shows potential for 10% EBITDA margins by year-end 2028, supported by advertising revenue growth, private label expansion, Chewy Health initiatives, fulfillment center automation, and customer value expansion.

High single-digit topline growth is expected to continue through 2026, driven by marketing efficiency, customer value creation, and cross-selling opportunities. The stock trades at 13x projected 2027 EBITDA, considered reasonable compared to peers.

In recent news, Chewy reported third-quarter results that surpassed consensus estimates for both revenue and EBITDA. The company also announced that its chief technology officer, Satish Mehta, intends to retire in February 2026.

title: Big Tech earnings to test AI rally as resurgent Alphabet takes lead
content:
By Aditya Soni

Jan 27 (Reuters) - Microsoft and Meta will kick off Big Tech earnings this week under pressure to prove that their costly bets on artificial intelligence can power another year of strong growth as a resurgent Alphabet takes the lead in the high-stakes race.

The companies, along with Amazon, are expected to lift their AI spending by 30% to more than $500 billion this year, an unprecedented outlay that will sharpen investor scrutiny.

Doubts have deepened whether Microsoft has squandered a first-mover advantage in AI it secured through its OpenAI investment. Meta too is on the hook to show payoffs from its expensive push into superintelligence.

Both stocks declined more than 6% in the last three months of 2025, while Amazon notched a small 5.1% gain after its November deal with OpenAI signaled that the largest cloud-computing provider in the U.S. was no longer an AI laggard.

Alphabet shares, though, surged about 29% in that period, following a strong reception of Google’s latest Gemini 3 model. The company also recently struck a deal to power Apple’s revamped Siri.

"Alphabet has the upper hand in the AI race as investors recognize that proprietary ecosystems, such as Apple and Search in Google, are tough to penetrate," said David Wagner, head of equities at Aptus Capital Advisors, a Big Tech investor.

"Like in the internet boom, the first-mover advantage doesn’t always win the marathon."

Microsoft and Meta will report earnings on Wednesday, while Alphabet and Amazon report next week.

AI BUBBLE FEARS REMAIN

In the October-December quarter, Google Cloud revenue growth likely quickened to 35% from 33.5% in the previous three months, according to LSEG.

Microsoft’s Azure is expected to post a 38.8% rise, slower than the 40% jump it reported in the preceding quarter. Amazon Web Services likely grew 21.1%, up from 20.2% in the prior period.

Still, doubts linger about the real-world benefits for the businesses adopting the technology, feeding into bubble fears that hounded the technology industry for much of last year.

More than half the respondents in a PwC survey of 4,454 CEOs earlier this month said they realized neither revenue nor cost benefits from investments in AI.

"For this not to be a bubble by definition, it requires that the benefits of this are much more evenly spread," Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella said at Davos.

Morgan Stanley analysts said sentiment toward Microsoft has shifted to "a wall of worry" due to growing competition for Azure and OpenAI, in which the company has a 27% stake.

Microsoft has said it is battling AI capacity constraints which it says are expected to last at least until June. As well, a surge in memory chip prices has dampened the outlook for the PC market, a critical driver for Microsoft’s personal computing business that houses Windows and Xbox consoles.

Overall, its revenue likely rose 15.3% to $80.27 billion in the October-December period, the slowest growth in three quarters.

GOOGLE GEMINI ADOPTION IN FOCUS

Alphabet is expected to benefit from rapid AI integration into search and a stable advertising market, with the company expected to report a 15.5% jump in revenue to $111.37 billion.

It also opened up a new revenue stream in October by agreeing to supply Anthropic - an AI startup it backs - with its AI chips called Tensor Processing Units. The deal, worth tens of billions of dollars, is a departure from Alphabet’s strategy of reserving these chips for internal use.

Meta’s efforts to improve ad search and recommendations with AI, meanwhile, are expected to have powered a 20.6% rise in revenue to $58.35 billion. But an expensive hiring spree for top AI talent is expected to slow profit growth to a near three-year low.

Amazon is expected to post a 12.5% rise in revenue, slightly slower than the preceding quarter as growth weakens at its North America retail business.

title: Amazon secures $581 million Pentagon cloud contract for Air Force
content:
Investing.com -- Amazon Web Services Inc. has been awarded a $581,318,585 firm-fixed-price contract to support the U.S. Air Force’s Cloud One Program, according to an announcement from the U.S. Department of War.

The Seattle-based company will provide Amazon Cloud Service to the Air Force and its customers. The contract work will be performed at designated facilities across the contiguous United States and is scheduled to be completed by December 7, 2028.

The Pentagon described the award as the result of a sole source acquisition, meaning Amazon was the only company considered for the contract.

The Air Force Life Cycle Management Center at Hanscom Air Force Base in Massachusetts is serving as the contracting activity for the agreement, which has been assigned the identifier FA8726-26-F-B004.

At the time of the award, the Air Force has obligated $3,497,937 in fiscal 2026 operations and maintenance funds toward the contract.

title: BMO Capital raises Novo Nordisk stock price target to $57 on market outlook
content:
The firm cited flat prescription trends (TRx) for Novo Nordisk’s weight loss drug Wegovy in the fourth quarter of 2025 compared to the third quarter, which contrasts with competitor Eli Lilly’s Zepbound that saw 17% quarter-over-quarter prescription growth in an expanding incretin market.

The firm expects U.S. prescriptions to remain largely flat in 2026 compared to 2025, resulting in negative year-over-year U.S. net sales growth due to gross-to-net erosion, based on management guidance for similar patient access in the U.S. in 2026.

BMO Capital projects that declining U.S. sales may be partially offset by growth outside the United States, yielding approximately 3% worldwide sales growth in 2026 according to their model. This projection aligns with Novo Nordisk’s historical performance as a prominent player in the Pharmaceuticals industry, with the company maintaining dividend payments for 37 consecutive years.

In other recent news, Novo Nordisk has maintained its price target of $46, as reiterated by BMO Capital, following discussions at a JPMorgan conference. Analyst Evan Seigerman from BMO Capital noted that 2025 posed significant challenges for the company, particularly due to increased competition from Eli Lilly and other GLP-1 compounders. Additionally, Amazon Pharmacy has announced it will start offering Novo Nordisk’s Wegovy weight-loss pill, intensifying competition in the weight management market. This move allows eligible customers to access the medication through insurance plans, with a cash-pay option available for those without coverage.

LifeMD Inc has also added Novo Nordisk’s Wegovy pill to its telehealth offerings, marking it as the first FDA-approved GLP-1 pill for weight management. The UK Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency is reviewing Novo Nordisk’s application for an oral 25 milligram version of Wegovy, with a decision expected by year’s end. Furthermore, Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly have announced price reductions for their obesity medications Wegovy and Mounjaro in China, with significant cuts reported in certain provinces. These developments reflect ongoing strategic adjustments by Novo Nordisk in response to a competitive market landscape.

title: Will AI investment keep soaring in 2026? Aubrey Capital weighs in.
content:
Investing.com - The latest quarterly earnings season has brought a fresh wave of enthusiasm around artificial intelligence, but also some worries around margin pressures from heavy spending on the infrastructure needed to power the nascent technology, according to analysts at Aubrey Capital Management.

Investors have been keen to see returns from massive spending binges, especially among mega-cap “hyperscaler” names who are racing to fold AI into their offerings.

Oracle has born the brunt of much of these fears, with the cloud software name sliding by almost 44% from a surge in September. At the time, Oracle shares popped on its announcement of a $300 billion contract with OpenAI, the company behind popular chatbot ChatGPT, but traders later balked at OpenAI’s ability to stand by the commitments and Oracle’s debt-funded build-out of data centers.

Writing in a note to clients, the Aubrey analysts argued that while AI investments were previously met with market cheers, “this trend has been broken, and not all capex is created equal.”

“Where companies such as the hyperscalers are using cash piles to invest, they are being rewarded, while those using debt are under severe scrutiny. Clearly investors are becoming more selective,” they said.

Still, the Aubrey analysts said much of the hyperscalers’ spending increases were “justified by very strong operational performance,” with software giant Microsoft and Google-parent Alphabet both raising their cloud revenues in the double-digit percentages. Facebook-owner Meta’s core advertising business, meanwhile, has grown by 26%, they noted.

“This type of performance has been an integral driver of AI capex spending, with hyperscaler operating cash flows accounting for 60% of AI spend,” the analysts wrote. “Given the strong performance of the hyperscalers, this has given investors renewed faith that the beneficiaries of all this spending will continue to perform well.”

Collectively, Meta, Microsoft, Alphabet, and e-commerce titan Amazon are expected to shell out more than $480 billion in capital expenditures in 2026, or roughly 60% of total industry-wide AI spending, the analysts said. They added that many expect the number to ramp up even more “as we close out the year.”

“This should shore up the fundamentals into the start of next year, but deeper questions remain about the speed of enterprise adoption and when the technology will be good enough to replace a wider range of the workforce,” the analysts said.

title: UBS explains why ’nostalgia is not an investment strategy’
content:
Investing.com -- UBS Global Wealth Management’s chief investment officer has cautioned investors against clinging to the old international order as geopolitical rivalry reshapes markets, urging a shift toward diversification and broader equity exposure.

"Nostalgia for the old international order is not an investment strategy," Mark Haefele wrote in UBS’ monthly letter, paraphrasing Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney’s Davos speech.

The wealth manager highlighted a more volatile world marked by the US administration’s "Donroe Doctrine," strained China-Japan relations, Iranian violence, and NATO fractures over Greenland and Ukraine, all pointing to geopolitical blocs seeking greater strategic autonomy.

Despite the turbulence, UBS maintained a positive market stance, forecasting the S&P 500 to reach 7,700 by year-end from 6,876 currently, with earnings per share growth of 12% for 2026. The brokerage expects global equities to rise over 10% by end-2026.

The recent equity rally has been narrow, with the "AI7,” - NVIDIA, Broadcom, AMD, Micron, Alphabet, Amazon, and Microsoft - appreciating roughly 200% over three years, about five times the gain of the rest of the S&P 500, according to UBS data.

The brokerage expects leadership to shift from semiconductor firms toward companies selling AI solutions to consumers and businesses.

Global AI capital expenditures are forecast to rise at an average annual rate of 25% from 2025 to 2030, reaching $1.3 trillion per annum.

The Ramp AI Index, measuring US businesses with paid AI subscriptions, rose to 47% in December, roughly doubling from a year earlier.

UBS identified government intervention as a near-term risk, particularly around affordability. A Politico poll in late 2025 found 56% of Americans consider high living costs a top issue.

However, with 62% of American households owning stocks according to a 2025 Gallup poll, the brokerage believes the administration will avoid broad market-negative policies.

President Trump’s Davos comments suggesting Congressional action on credit card rate caps, rather than executive orders, signal limited appetite for intervention, UBS said.

The drive for strategic autonomy is creating beneficiaries across sectors. The US secured a $500 billion Taiwan investment deal, including $250 billion in semiconductors and AI capacity.

Europe’s Chips Act targets 20% global market share by 2030, while China’s Five-Year Plan includes four trillion yuan for grid investment, a 40% increase.

Defense spending is accelerating, with German outlays projected at 3.5% of GDP by decade-end and Trump calling for a $1.5 trillion 2027 military budget, up from $901 billion in 2026.

UBS upgraded US consumer discretionary stocks and emerging-market bonds to "Attractive," forecasting European earnings acceleration of 7% in 2026 and 18% in 2027 after three years of stagnation.

The brokerage noted geopolitical concerns have driven foreign holdings of US Treasuries lower while boosting gold, which it continues to favor alongside quality bonds for portfolio diversification in an increasingly fragmented world.

title: ’Jobs, jobs, jobs’ the AI mantra in Davos as fears take back seat
content:
By Jeffrey Dastin

DAVOS, Switzerland, Jan 23 (Reuters) - Biting cold, political tensions and doubts about artificial intelligence did nothing to curb the enthusiasm of business leaders in Davos over technology’s ability to create jobs.

Top executives at the World Economic Forum’s annual meeting said that while jobs would disappear, new ones would spring up, with two telling Reuters that AI would be used as an excuse by companies which were planning layoffs anyway.

Flag bearers of AI’s trillion-dollar expansion, including chip titan Jensen Huang, said the technology heralded higher pay and more jobs for plumbers, electricians and steelworkers.

"Energy is creating jobs. Chips industry is creating jobs. The infrastructure layer is creating jobs," the Nvidia CEO told the meeting in the Swiss mountain resort.

"Jobs, jobs, jobs," added Huang.

That optimism contrasted with a potential trade row that had reverberated through Davos until U.S. President Donald Trump struck a deal to call off tariffs and avert a security decoupling with Europe over Greenland.

But scepticism over AI simmered below the surface.

Delegates discussed how chatbots could lead consumers to psychosis and suicide, while labour union leaders questioned the cost of recent technology gains.

"AI is being sold as a productivity tool, which often means doing more with fewer workers," said Christy Hoffman, general secretary of the 20-million-strong UNI Global Union.

TOWARD RETURNS

Matthew Prince, CEO of internet security company Cloudflare, said during an interview with Reuters in a mountain restaurant above Davos that AI would keep advancing and that scrappy developers could overcome market or funding blips.

Prince, who said he sticks to six-minute chair-lift meetings rather than windowless conference rooms during Davos, warned that AI could become so dominant in the future that small businesses are eviscerated while autonomous agents handle consumers’ shopping requests.

In recent years, businesses have griped about how to go beyond ill-fated AI pilots and capitalize on an AI craze started by ChatGPT in 2022.

Rob Thomas, IBM’s chief commercial officer, said AI has reached a stage where there can be a return on investment. "You can truly start to automate tasks and business processes," he told Reuters.

However, PwC said only one in eight CEOs recently surveyed by the advisory firm believed AI was lowering costs and delivering more elusive revenue. And questions remain about what business model can make up for AI’s enormous expenses.

Cathinka Wahlstrom, chief commercial officer at BNY, said AI has paid off by shortening the amount of research time for onboarding a new client from two days to 10 minutes.

And in the past month and a half, projects that networking company Cisco had viewed as too tedious to take on - requiring 19 man-years of work - were now getting done in a couple of weeks, its President Jeetu Patel said in an interview.

"The way in which we code actually was rethought," said Patel, adding that software developers should embrace AI not just for productivity but to "be relevant" in the long run.

HEADCOUNT FLAT

Rob Goldstein, BlackRock’s chief operating officer, told a media roundtable that the world’s biggest asset manager had secured nearly $700 billion in net new client assets last year, viewing AI as a means to business expansion rather than to workforce reductions.

"We’re very focused on keeping our headcount flat as we continue to grow," Goldstein said.

Meanwhile, Amazon.com is planning a second round of cuts next week as part of a broader goal of slashing some 30,000 corporate jobs, two people familiar with the matter told Reuters.

Part of the reason anxiety about jobs persists despite corporate assurances is if workers have little say in the rollout of AI, said Luc Triangle, general secretary of the International Trade Union Confederation.

In these conditions, workers see AI "as a threat", he said.

For billionaire philanthropist and Microsoft co-founder Bill Gates, the world needs to "get ready for the opportunities and disruption that AI will bring".

"Your economy gets more productive,” Gates told Reuters. “That’s typically a good thing.”

Gates cited taxing AI activities as one potential idea for assisting workers, while calling on politicians to get more familiar with the technology.

"There certainly are problems, but they’re all solvable problems," Gates added about AI more generally.

Davos largely wrapped up on Thursday with optimism from Elon Musk, founder of SpaceX and Tesla CEO, who talked about his goal to protect civilisation and make it interplanetary.

"For quality of life, it is actually better to err on the side of being an optimist and wrong, rather than a pessimist and right," Musk told a packed congress hall, before he was escorted out via a kitchen, dodging waiting reporters.

title: Dow ends lower after topsy-turvy week, as Intel’s outlook weighs on market sentiment
content:
By David French

Jan 23 (Reuters) - The Dow Jones Industrial Average finished down on Friday, while the S&P 500 ended largely unchanged, as investors’ risk appetite was dimmed at the end of a topsy-turvy week by Intel’s plunge on a downbeat outlook.

All three Wall Street benchmarks had rebounded in the past two sessions following Tuesday’s sharp selloff triggered by U.S. President Donald Trump’s threats to impose tariffs on European allies, an effort to pressure them to accept his claims to Greenland.

On Friday, the Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 285.30 points, or 0.58%, to 49,098.71. The S&P 500 edged up 2.26 points, or 0.03%, to 6,915.61, and the Nasdaq Composite gained 65.23 points, or 0.28%, to 23,501.24.

Even the Nasdaq’s Friday gains were not enough to rescue the benchmarks from having a down week, with the S&P 500 off by 0.36%, the Dow lower by 0.53% and the Nasdaq slipping by 0.06%.

Despite the week’s limited pullback, investors appeared to remain confident that while geopolitical-induced volatility is a present danger, the overall state of the American economy continues to be robust.

"When we think about what it means from an investor’s standpoint, we feel pretty good about where we are today," said Jason Blackwell, chief investment strategist at Focus Partners Wealth.

He noted that volatility was expected this year, given the midterms later in 2026. However, corporate earnings are expected to continue to come in strong, and the economy is going along fine.

"We’re feeling pretty good, but mindful we might have some significant twists and turns throughout the rest of the year," Blackwell added.

THE ’SHOW-ME’ SEASON

One twist on Friday, which weighed on market sentiment, was chipmaker Intel. Its shares sank 17% after the company forecast quarterly revenue and profit below market estimates, saying it struggled to satisfy demand for its server chips used in AI data centers.

With many technology and semiconductor companies still trading at sky-high valuations, 2026 is viewed by many as the year where the huge excitement for the artificial intelligence trend, and the huge amounts of capital expenditure to attain it, need to start showing up as corporate revenue.

Julian McManus, portfolio manager on the Global Alpha Equity team at Janus Henderson, noted how last week’s earnings from TSMC, the world’s main producer of advanced AI chips, could portend well for the latest earnings from the space.

“Going into results, we’re going to be in a ‘show-me’ period, where you have to actually put up the revenue growth to justify the run-up in stocks,” he said. “It’s going to be a period of the haves versus the have-nots, and I personally don’t see Intel being in the haves.”

MAG 7 EARNINGS TEST

This show-me point will be particularly pertinent for investors next week, with earnings on deck from many of the so-called Magnificent Seven stocks, including Apple, Tesla and Microsoft.

On Friday, most of the megacaps rose, with Microsoft, Meta and Amazon up between 1.7% and 3.3%. Nvidia gained 1.5% after Bloomberg News reported Chinese officials have told Alibaba, Tencent and ByteDance they can prepare orders for Nvidia’s H200 AI chips.

Of the S&P sub-sectors, seven ended in positive territory, led by the 0.9% increase in materials.

The energy index rose 0.6% on Friday, to its third successive record closing high. It was also the top performing sub-index for the week, while its 10.1% advance so far in 2026 is unmatched.

The number of shares changing hands on U.S. exchanges on Friday was 17.34 billion shares, compared with the 17.07 billion average over the last 20 trading days.

title: Citizens downgrades Trade Desk on rising Amazon competition
content:
Investing.com -- Citizens downgraded The Trade Desk to Market Perform from Market Outperform, saying rising competition has capped the upside in the shares.

Competitive pressure among demand-side platforms is intensifying as generative AI lowers switching costs by simplifying programmatic ad buying. That shift weakens The Trade Desk’s historical advantages and favors rivals with access to proprietary data.

Amazon is a growing competitive threat given its access to the industry’s largest retail media data set. Data supports stronger ad targeting and closed-loop measurement, which are becoming more important differentiators as AI reduces operational complexity across platforms.

There is structural pressure on open web advertising. Open web ad spends, excluding connected TV and digital audio, continues to slow as walled gardens gain share of digital ad budgets.

AI-driven search and AI Overviews are also weighing on publisher traffic and monetization, which Citizens sees as a headwind to impression growth and ad spend.

Citizens said several factors could pressure The Trade Desk’s take rates into 2026. It pointed to Amazon offering lower fees to attract advertisers and said The Trade Desk has begun negotiating fees and offering incentives to some clients in response.

It also highlighted the September 2025 launch of Audience Unlimited, which shifted data pricing to tiered, impression-based fees of about 3.3% to 4.4% of impression costs. While broader data adoption could lift revenue, Citizens said heavier use by existing large customers could dilute take rates.

Citizens said the stock trades at about 11.2x 2027 estimated EBITDA, in line with peers. With competition rising and few near-term catalysts to improve financial performance, the brokerage said the risk-reward profile appears balanced at current levels.

title: The Trade Desk stock rating downgraded by Citizens on competitive concerns

The downgrade reflects Citizens’ view that competitive intensity is rising for The Trade Desk, with generative AI technology potentially lowering switching costs across demand-side platforms (DSPs) by simplifying the programmatic buying user interface.

The research firm also pointed to "a lack of near-term catalysts that could meaningfully improve financial performance" for The Trade Desk, leading to a balanced risk/reward profile at current stock levels.

The downgrade comes as The Trade Desk faces potential challenges in maintaining its competitive position in the digital advertising technology landscape amid evolving market dynamics and technological changes.

In other recent news, The Trade Desk’s financial outlook has seen various updates from analysts. BofA Securities lowered its price target for The Trade Desk to $40, maintaining an Underperform rating, while expecting fourth-quarter 2025 results to align with guidance on revenue and adjusted EBITDA. Meanwhile, Cannonball Research noted that the company’s OpenPath product now contributes about 10% to its top line in Q3 2025, reiterating a Buy rating with a $70 price target due to increased adoption by major connected TV publishers. MoffettNathanson upgraded The Trade Desk from Sell to Neutral, setting a price target of $39, following a significant share price decline. Stifel reiterated a Buy rating, naming The Trade Desk its top pick in the Ad Tech sector, as the company navigates past challenges with its Kokai platform and the 2025 U.S. Presidential election cycle. Additionally, Guggenheim reduced its price target to $50, citing competitive pressures in the digital advertising market but maintained a Buy rating, seeing an attractive risk-reward profile. These developments reflect a mixed but cautiously optimistic analyst outlook on The Trade Desk’s performance and market position.

title: Amazon plans second round of corporate job cuts next week, Reuters reports
content:
Investing.com -- Amazon.com Inc (NASDAQ:AMZN) is preparing a second wave of corporate layoffs next week as part of an ongoing initiative to trim its global workforce by roughly 30,000 employees. According to reporting from Reuters, the upcoming cuts are expected to mirror the scale of the 14,000 positions eliminated last October.

The planned reductions could begin as early as Tuesday, according to Reuters, citing people familiar with the matter who spoke on the condition of anonymity. These moves represent a significant step toward the company’s goal of streamlining its professional ranks after years of rapid expansion.

While an internal letter previously linked the October reductions to the transformative power of artificial intelligence, CEO Andy Jassy has offered a different rationale for the restructuring. During a third-quarter earnings call, Jassy clarified that the shift was "not really financially driven and it’s not even really AI-driven."

The chief executive pointed toward organizational friction and a desire to return to the company’s leaner roots as the primary catalysts for the change. “You end up with a lot more people than what you had before, and you end up with a lot more layers," Jassy told analysts during the call.

The total reduction of 30,000 jobs would represent nearly 10% of Amazon’s corporate workforce, though it remains a fraction of the firm’s 1.58 million total employees. Most of the company’s staff continues to be concentrated in its massive network of fulfillment centers and warehouses.

This series of cuts marks the largest headcount reduction in the online retailer’s three-decade history, surpassing the 27,000 jobs eliminated in 2022. The strategy reflects a broader corporate trend of using technology and restructuring to reduce bureaucracy and reliance on human labor.

Jobs in Amazon’s AWS, retail, Prime Video, and HR divisions are set to be impacted, the sources told Reuters, noting that plans could change. Amazon stock rose 0.2% in after-hours trade following the report.

title: Exclusive-Amazon plans thousands more corporate job cuts next week, sources say
content:
By Greg Bensinger

SAN FRANCISCO, Jan 22 (Reuters) - Amazon is planning a second round of job cuts next week as part of its broader goal of trimming some 30,000 corporate workers, according to two people familiar with the matter.

The company in October cut some 14,000 white collar jobs, about half of the 30,000 target first reported by Reuters. The total this time is expected to be roughly the same as last year and could begin as soon as Tuesday, the people said, who asked not to be identified because they were not authorized to discuss Amazon’s plans.

An Amazon spokesperson declined to comment.

title: Nvidia’s Huang: AI boom will create six-figure trade jobs
content:
Investing.com -- Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang emphasized that the artificial intelligence boom will create high-paying trade jobs while speaking at the World Economic Forum in Davos on Wednesday.

"This is the largest infrastructure build-out in human history that’s going to create a lot of jobs," Huang said during his speech. "It’s wonderful that the jobs are related to trade craft, and we’re going to have plumbers and electricians and construction and steel workers, and network technicians, and people who install and fit out the equipment."

The tech executive noted a significant boom in these sectors, with salaries nearly doubling. "We’re talking about six-figure salaries for people who are building chip factories or computer factories or AI factories," he added.

Huang’s comments come as several major companies including Amazon, Salesforce, Accenture, and Lufthansa cited AI as a reason for employee layoffs in 2025.

"Everybody should be able to make a great living. You don’t need to have a PhD in computer science to do so," Huang said, striking an optimistic tone about AI’s impact on the labor market despite concerns about automation eliminating white-collar positions.

title: Jefferies reiterates Buy rating on Microsoft stock amid AI investments
content:
Jefferies believes Microsoft is positioned to drive upside through execution against its massive backlog as it ramps capacity significantly this year, highlighting the company’s recent $250 billion OpenAI commitment and $30 billion Anthropic agreement. These investments come as Microsoft maintains strong revenue growth of 15.59% over the last twelve months, with the tech giant set to report its next earnings in just 6 days on January 28.

The firm considers Microsoft’s valuation attractive at 23x CY27 EPS, which it notes is below hyperscalers Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) at 24x and Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOGL) at 25x despite Microsoft’s superior visibility, remaining performance obligations (RPO), and clear AI monetization vectors.

Jefferies expects Microsoft’s fiscal second quarter RPO to show the largest sequential step-up ever, driven by inclusion of the OpenAI and Anthropic agreements, reinforcing what it calls "unprecedented multi-year demand visibility" for Azure and Microsoft 365 Commercial.

In other recent news, Microsoft has secured a $170 million contract from the U.S. Department of War to provide cloud services for the Air Force’s Cloud One Program. This contract will utilize Microsoft’s Azure cloud service offerings and will be executed at various facilities across the United States. Meanwhile, Stifel has reiterated its Buy rating on Microsoft, highlighting the company’s substantial $500 million annual investment in Anthropic’s AI models as a significant commitment to expanding its artificial intelligence capabilities. Additionally, Bernstein has included Microsoft in its 2026 software picks, emphasizing the growing investor interest in artificial intelligence and its potential impact on enterprise software. On the other hand, TD Cowen has adjusted its price target for Microsoft to $625, down from $655, while maintaining a Buy rating. The firm noted stable or strengthening demand for GPU and CPU infrastructure, with expectations of a potential increase in their Azure estimate. These developments reflect Microsoft’s ongoing strategic initiatives and investments in cloud and AI technologies.

title: Altman pursues Middle East capital as OpenAI seeks $800 billion valuation - Report
content:
Investing.com -- OpenAI Chief Executive Officer Sam Altman has been meeting with top investors in the Middle East to line up funding for a new investment round. This capital injection could total at least $50 billion, according to a Wednesday report from Bloomberg citing people familiar with the matter.

Altman recently visited the region to hold discussions with leading state-backed funds in Abu Dhabi, the people said.

The ChatGPT maker is currently aiming for a valuation between $750 billion and $830 billion, according to Bloomberg’s sources. However, the talks remain in the early stages and the final figures could still change.

OpenAI has also held recent discussions with Amazon.com Inc. to raise at least $10 billion, Bloomberg News has previously reported. While the startup apparently declined to comment on the current round, previous reports from The Information suggest the company is aggressively pursuing tens of billions in new capital.

The company has already raised billions to finance the immense costs of specialized chips, data centers, and technical talent. OpenAI has committed to spending more than $1.4 trillion on AI infrastructure in the coming years, including a $100 billion UAE-based data center under the ’Stargate’ data center building initiative.

The startup was most recently valued at $500 billion in the fall of 2025 during a deal that allowed employee share sales. This potential new round would represent a significant leap in the company’s market position and capital reserves. It may also reinvigorate investor sentiment in the AI trade as fears regarding the evolving technology have roiled markets in the past months.

title: Bezos’ Blue Origin to deploy thousands of satellites for new ’TeraWave’ communications network
content:
By Joey Roulette

WASHINGTON, Jan 21 (Reuters) - Jeff Bezos’ space company Blue Origin on Wednesday announced a plan to deploy 5,408 satellites in space for a communications network that will serve data centers, governments and businesses, jumping into a satellite constellation market dominated by Elon Musk’s SpaceX.

Deployment of satellites is planned to begin in the last quarter of 2027, Blue Origin said, adding the network is designed to have "data speeds of up to 6 Tbps anywhere on Earth."

That speed, possible with the satellites’ planned optical communications, is extreme by consumer standards and would make the network key for data processing and large-scale government programs. Blue Origin said the network is meant to serve a maximum of roughly 100,000 customers.

The reveal of TeraWave coincides with a space industry rush to build data centers in space that can meet the soaring demand for large-scale AI data processing, which on Earth requires immense energy and resources as adoption of the technology expands.

The planned network adds another satellite constellation linked to Bezos, executive chairman of Amazon, which is in an early phase of deploying Leo - a network formerly called Project Kuiper involving 3,200 satellites providing internet to consumers and businesses.

BEZOS LAUNCHES NEW STARLINK RIVAL

Musk’s Starlink network of roughly 10,000 satellites is farthest ahead in a global push to put internet infrastructure in space, where swarms of low-orbiting satellites offer more security and higher connection speeds than traditional, unitary satellites farther out in space.

The SpaceX CEO has said he plans to build data centers in space, complementing the Starlink network, while Bezos has predicted such space-based centers will be commonplace in orbit within the next 10 to 20 years.

Starlink, which has reported more than six million customers across at least 140 countries, targets individual consumers, businesses, governments and, with its Starshield variant, U.S. national security agencies. Amazon Leo, with 180 satellites in space so far, has a similar customer strategy.

A handful of Chinese companies are swiftly deploying similar satellite networks to get up to pace with Starlink, which has proved key for remote communications and geopolitical conflicts.

China is developing new reusable rockets that can loft thousands of these satellites over the next several years for lower costs, a playbook first written by Musk’s SpaceX and its reusable Falcon 9 rocket.

Blue Origin’s reusable New Glenn rocket, which has launched twice but has been slow to achieve a rapid flight rate, will likely be a vital part of TeraWave’s deployment.

Unlike Starlink, the planned network appears off-limits to individual consumers, according to Blue Origin’s statement.

"What makes TeraWave different? It is purpose-built for enterprise customers," said Blue Origin CEO Dave Limp on X.

Blue Origin’s statement said "TeraWave enterprise-grade user and gateway terminals can be rapidly deployed worldwide and interface with existing high-capacity infrastructure, providing additional route diversity and strengthening overall network resilience," leaving unclear what types of existing infrastructure the network can work with.

A Blue Origin spokeswoman did not immediately return a request for comment.

title: Evercore ISI reiterates Outperform rating on Amazon stock, $335 target
content:
The research firm notes that Amazon’s unit-cost curve shows meaningful improvement compared to 2021, with projections extending through the third quarter of 2025. Recent quarters have shown a modest rebound in cost structure, highlighting labor and last-mile freight costs as emerging key factors. This cost optimization contributes to Amazon’s solid financial performance, with revenue reaching $691.33 billion in the last twelve months and growing at 11.48%.

Evercore ISI observes that Amazon’s network is transitioning from "regionalization as routing" to "regionalization-by-design," with sortation increasingly integrated into newer fulfillment center formats to reduce touchpoints and improve throughput.

The firm points to increasingly sophisticated outbound and last-mile infrastructure, including mega sortation hubs, smaller regional sortation hubs, delivery-station automation, and an expanded station footprint with rural formats. These developments reinforce Amazon’s ability to lower costs and reduce reliance on external shipping partners.

According to Evercore ISI, the near-term question for Amazon centers on whether automation cadence and productivity gains can outpace labor inflation while the company continues to build out its network and internalize more of its shipping operations.

In other recent news, Amazon announced the launch of the AWS European Sovereign Cloud, a new independent cloud infrastructure specifically designed for Europe, with plans for a €7.8 billion investment. Additionally, Amazon is reportedly seeking to reduce payments to suppliers on its e-commerce platform, aiming to reverse concessions made during the previous U.S. administration’s tariff policies. Meanwhile, Bernstein SocGen Group reiterated its Outperform rating on Amazon, citing strong growth potential for 2026 and maintaining a $300.00 price target. In contrast, Raymond James lowered its price target for Amazon to $260 due to concerns about AI-related risks but maintained an Outperform rating.

In other developments, Hasbro appointed Doug Bowser, former President and COO of Nintendo of America, and Carla Vernón, CEO of The Honest Company, to its Board of Directors. Bowser’s tenure at Nintendo was marked by significant growth, driven by the Nintendo Switch platform. These appointments are part of Hasbro’s ongoing efforts to strengthen its leadership team. These recent developments highlight strategic moves by both Amazon and Hasbro to position themselves for future growth and adaptation in their respective markets.

title: Tariffs starting to bump up product prices, Amazon CEO tells CNBC
content:
Jan 20 (Reuters) - Amazon.com is starting to see an uptick in product prices on its e-commerce platform as sellers respond to cost pressures stemming from U.S. President Donald Trump’s tariffs, the tech giant’s CEO, Andy Jassy, told CNBC on Tuesday.

The company had pulled forward its inventory shipments early last year and urged third-party sellers to bring in more stock ahead of time to circumvent tariff-driven surges in shipping costs, but "that supply has run out in the fall," Jassy said in the interview at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland.

"(We’re starting) to see some of the tariffs creep into some prices. Some sellers are deciding that they’re passing on those higher costs to consumers, some are deciding that they’ll absorb it to drive demand, and some are doing something in between. So you’re starting to see more of that impact," Jassy said.

Still, consumers have been largely resilient, with people continuing to shop and looking for bargains, Jassy said, adding that shoppers are a "little bit more hesitant" on higher-priced discretionary purchases.

"Amazon’s consumers overall have fared well. But we’ll have to see what happens in 2026."

The company has said over the past year that it was seeing very little impact on consumer behavior and product prices from tariffs. It has doubled down on expanding product categories and speeding up delivery timelines to shield demand.

Meanwhile, rising prices and mounting cost-of-living concerns in the U.S. have emerged as key questions for Trump to address ahead of the midterm elections this year.

U.S. tariffs are also a crucial talking point for global leaders in Davos this week.

Amazon’s shares were down 2.7% in early trading amid weakness in the broader market.

Separately, speaking at Davos to CNBC, Coca-Cola’s outgoing CEO said the company was "relatively immune to tariffs". The company is exposed to some higher costs from aluminum and resin, but has said over the past year that tariffs were "manageable" for the company because of its largely localized manufacturing processes.

title: Streaming captures nearly half of US TV viewing in December on ’Stranger Things’ boost
content:
Jan 20 (Reuters) - Streaming services captured nearly half of all television viewership in the U.S. in December, powered by Netflix’s Christmas Day NFL games and the highly anticipated final season of "Stranger Things," audience measurement firm Nielsen reported on Tuesday.

With a record 47.5% of total TV usage in the U.S., including a staggering 55.1 billion viewing minutes on Christmas Day, streaming services cemented their dominance over broadcast and cable, Nielsen said in its monthly report, The Gauge.

Google’s YouTube alone accounted for 12.7% of all television viewing last month, representing the largest audience share for a streaming service.

The data reflects the ongoing transformation of the television industry, as viewers increasingly gravitate toward on-demand content and live sports migrate to digital platforms.

On Christmas Day alone, streaming commanded 54% of all TV usage, the largest single-day share ever recorded by the category.

The two NFL games on Netflix , new "Stranger Things" episodes, and Amazon Prime Video’s late NFL game combined to propel the demand.

Netflix viewership was up 10% from November, as "Stranger Things" generated over 15 billion viewing minutes to become December’s most-watched streaming title.

Broadcast accounted for 21.4% of TV viewership in December, while cable had a 20.2% share, Nielsen said.

The shift is driving consolidation in the sector, with Netflix bidding $82.7 billion to acquire Warner Bros Discovery’s studio and streaming assets.

title: Hasbro appoints former Nintendo and Honest Company execs to board
content:
PAWTUCKET, R.I. - Hasbro, Inc. (NASDAQ:HAS) announced Tuesday the appointment of Doug Bowser, retired President and Chief Operating Officer of Nintendo of America, and Carla Vernón, CEO of The Honest Company, to its Board of Directors.

Bowser served in various leadership roles at Nintendo of America for over a decade, including as President and COO from April 2019 until his recent retirement. During his tenure, he oversaw a period of significant growth driven by the Nintendo Switch platform and the launch of Nintendo Switch 2. He previously spent eight years at Electronic Arts and 23 years at Procter & Gamble in sales leadership positions.

"Doug and Carla bring extensive leadership experience across consumer brands and franchise management," said Rich Stoddart, Chair of Hasbro’s Board of Directors, in a press release statement.

Hasbro CEO Chris Cocks noted that their expertise would support the company’s "Playing To Win" strategy as it continues to develop play experiences across its portfolio, which includes brands such as Magic: The Gathering, Dungeons & Dragons, Monopoly, and Transformers.

The toy and game company, founded over 164 years ago, reaches more than 500 million consumers globally through physical and digital games, toys, licensed products, and entertainment properties.

In other recent news, Amazon.com has announced the general availability of its AWS European Sovereign Cloud, a new cloud infrastructure specifically designed for Europe. This development involves a significant investment of €7.8 billion and marks Amazon’s commitment to expanding its cloud services within the European Union. Meanwhile, Amazon is reportedly seeking to cut payments to suppliers on its e-commerce platform, aiming to reverse concessions made to mitigate tariff impacts from the previous U.S. administration.

In terms of analyst activity, Bernstein SocGen Group has reiterated its Outperform rating for Amazon, maintaining a price target of $300 and highlighting strong growth potential for 2026. Raymond James, however, has lowered its price target for Amazon to $260, citing concerns over AI-related risks that might affect Amazon’s position in the e-commerce landscape. On a more optimistic note, TD Cowen has increased its price target for Amazon to $315, encouraged by positive trends in Amazon’s advertising business, which is expected to capture a larger share of advertising budgets. These developments reflect a mix of strategic initiatives and varied analyst perspectives on Amazon’s future performance.

title: CommVault Systems stock price target maintained at $144 by Cantor Fitzgerald
content:
The firm expects largely in-line fiscal third-quarter 2026 results, led by Software as a Service (SaaS), with muted Term License growth (approximately one-third of revenue) due to duration effects, which remain a key focus amid recent volatility. With earnings scheduled for January 27 (in 7 days), investors will be watching closely as the company maintains impressive 81.55% gross profit margins.

Cantor Fitzgerald believes net new constant currency Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR) of $45 million in the December quarter appears achievable, if not conservative, given ongoing cyber and data resiliency momentum in the market.

Channel checks were stable sequentially, though sales cycles remain elongated and competition intense, according to the firm. A large partner recently reduced calendar year 2026 growth expectations to the mid-teens from prior plans of 20% growth.

The firm does not expect commentary on out-year growth or ARR targets for fiscal year 2027 following the CFO departure in early December 2025, noting fiscal year 2026 ARR targets have already been met and current expectations are for relatively meaningful deceleration in ARR growth in fiscal year 2027.

In other recent news, Commvault Systems has announced its new Cloud Unified Data Vault service, which aims to extend protection capabilities to data stored using the S3 protocol. This cloud-native service is designed to bring S3-based application and AI data under a unified protection framework. Commvault also revealed a partnership with AWS to serve as a launch partner for the AWS European Sovereign Cloud, expected to open in Brandenburg, Germany by the end of 2025. Additionally, Commvault has entered into a partnership with Pinecone to enhance cyber resilience for AI vector databases, which are crucial for retrieval-augmented generation AI systems.

On the financial front, Guggenheim has lowered its price target for Commvault Systems from $220.00 to $200.00, while maintaining a Buy rating. The firm cites higher uncertainty amid the company’s business model transition but expects Commvault to exceed baseline constant currency Total NNARR in the fiscal third quarter of 2026. Meanwhile, in a separate development, Goldman Sachs downgraded Clarivate from Buy to Neutral, expressing concerns about the company’s Value Creation Plan. These recent developments highlight strategic moves and analyst adjustments impacting both Commvault and Clarivate.

This article was generated with the support of AI and reviewed by an editor. For more information see our T&C.

title: Jefferies says it’s "hard to understand" why Tesla is delaying robotaxi scaling
content:
Investing.com - On a recent clear and sunny day in San Francisco, analysts from Jefferies said they took to the streets to test robotaxi services from a range of companies.

Performance between Amazon’s self-driving unit Zoox, Google’s Waymo and Tesla were all similar in terms of "waiting time and driving behavior," they said.

On a comparable circuit, the cost for Waymo and Zoox was roughly $14 but "significantly lower" for Tesla at $6.25, the analysts including Philippe Houchois and Johnson Wan added.

"We found pick-up and delivery locations a bit erratic, with passengers having to walk to meet vehicles and odd stopping points," they said.

Overall, however, the analysts said the "technology experience" in the U.S. and in China "feels ready," even as both business models and capital intensity "remain unsettled."

For Tesla, in particular, the electric vehicle giant’s Full Self-Driving, or FSD, 14.2 robotaxi showed "very impressive" results in both highway and urban conditions, with accuracy and driving confidence high, the analysts said.

With that in mind, they noted that it is "hard to understand" why Tesla continues to delay scaling up its use of robotaxis in San Francisco or other American cities like Austin, where a security driver is no longer required. In the U.S. state of Nevada, the FSD 14.2 has been granted approval to operate without a human operator as well.

They suggested that the requirement of lidar sensors for self-driving cars could be one reason why Tesla has yet to roll out robotaxis on a bigger scale.

"Since cost is no longer a hurdle, adopting Lidar would look more an admission of ’defeat’ as integrating lidar should not be a major technical challenge," the analysts wrote.

Lidar, which stands for light detection and ranging, helps self-driving and driver-assistance systems create a three-dimensional map of the road by bouncing light pulses off of reflected objects. Tesla’s electric vehicles do not utilize lidars, a technology that CEO Elon Musk has referred to as "expensive appendices."

By using lidar, the idea that Tesla could turn its vehicles fitted with its Hardware 4 platform into a fleet of robotaxis may be "undermine[d]" as well, the Jefferies analysts said.

title: Expert flags split approaches among tech players in quantum computing
content:
Investing.com -- Experts say the push to develop quantum computing is unfolding through sharply divided strategies among major technology companies, reflecting unresolved technical challenges and differing views on how the technology should be built and delivered.

In a discussion with a quantum computing expert hosted by BMO Capital Markets, the expert said quantum computing remains at an early stage, with progress constrained by difficulties in error correction, fault tolerance and scalability.

Quantum computing uses principles of quantum physics to perform calculations that differ from classical computing, but the expert said broader adoption will depend on overcoming foundational technical limits.

The expert identified error correction as a central barrier, saying quantum states are highly fragile and can be disrupted by minor environmental changes such as heat. Without effective error correction, errors accumulate rapidly and prevent quantum algorithms from running long enough to produce meaningful results.

The expert said the industry is still in the early stages of developing stable, error-corrected logical qubits, which are required for fault-tolerant systems. Some providers have demonstrated only a limited number of logical qubits, while many others have not yet shown proven results.

The expert said these constraints have led to multiple competing hardware approaches, known as modalities, each with trade-offs. Superconducting qubits, used by companies such as IBM and Google, allow fast operations and scalable manufacturing but require extreme cooling and face networking limitations.

Ion and atomic qubits, used by IonQ, offer strong qubit connectivity but operate more slowly and become harder to scale physically. Photonic qubits, which encode information in particles of light, align naturally with fiber-optic networks but face challenges in building large, error-corrected systems.

According to the expert, ion and atomic systems have made greater progress in error correction than superconducting systems, with tens of logical qubits reported.

The expert said a dominant modality could emerge within the next five years but noted uncertainty around whether companies committed to superconducting systems could shift strategies if ion and atomic approaches gain traction.

Differences in approach extend to how major technology companies position themselves in the quantum market. The expert said IBM has focused on superconducting systems for several years and remains a leader, though its roadmap may face challenges related to scale and error correction.

Microsoft was described as having pulled back from developing its own quantum systems, instead emphasizing cloud platforms and third-party technology stacks.

Amazon was described as having a less mature hardware effort and focusing more on cloud-based quantum platforms rather than internal system development.

Google was described by the expert as more committed to quantum computing than Microsoft or Amazon but oriented toward academic research rather than near-term production use cases.

IonQ was identified as a leader in ion and atomic qubits, while the expert questioned D-Wave’s current capabilities, particularly how much of its offering relies on classical computing rather than quantum processing.

The expert said current quantum computing use cases are largely limited to scientific and academic research, with broader commercial applications expected to require hundreds to tens of thousands of logical qubits. Governments were cited as among the few buyers of quantum systems today.

The expert also said organizations may prefer accessing quantum computing through cloud services rather than owning and operating systems, given the complexity of maintaining hardware and the need for specialized expertise.

title: US FTC to scrutinize Big Tech’s talent acquisition deals, Bloomberg News reports
content:
Jan 16 (Reuters) - The U.S. Federal Trade Commission is scrutinizing big tech firms that hire employees of a startup instead of buying the companies outright, Bloomberg News reported on Friday, citing the agency’s Chairman Andrew Ferguson.

The growing practice, in which big tech firms pay for startups’ technology and talent without buying the company, is seen as a way to sidestep antitrust review.

"We are beginning to examine these acqui-hires to make sure they are not an attempt to get around" the agency’s merger review process, Ferguson said in an interview with Bloomberg Television.

Last month, Nvidia agreed to license chip technology from startup Groq and hire away its CEO Jonathan Ross, a veteran of Alphabet’s Google.

In similar recent deals, Microsoft’s top AI executive came through a $650 million deal with a startup that was billed as a licensing fee, and Meta spent $15 billion to hire Scale AI’s CEO without acquiring the firm.

Amazon also hired away founders from Adept AI. The deals have faced scrutiny by regulators, though none has yet been unwound.

In the interview, Ferguson blamed the Biden administration’s aggressive antitrust enforcement for prompting companies to ramp up this practice.

U.S. President Donald Trump fired the agency’s two Democratic commissioners last year, sparking a U.S. Supreme Court case that could tighten the president’s control over government agencies designed by Congress to be somewhat insulated from politics.

title: 7 Media & Telecom Stocks JPMorgan Favors for 2026
content:
Investing.com -- JPMorgan’s latest U.S. Analyst Focus List highlights seven standout performers in the Media & Telecom sector. These companies have been identified by the bank’s equity analysts as top investment ideas across growth, income, and value strategies.

Alphabet features on JPMorgan’s list as one of the bank’s preferred media and telecom names. The Google parent company continues to demonstrate strength across its core search business while expanding its footprint in cloud computing and artificial intelligence initiatives.

RBC Capital raised its price target on Alphabet to $375 from $315, maintaining an Outperform rating. The company also announced it is upgrading Gmail with new Gemini AI features designed to help users manage their inboxes more efficiently.

Amazon is also included among JPMorgan’s top picks. While known primarily for e-commerce, the company’s media and telecom operations, including Amazon Prime Video, AWS, and its growing advertising business, have positioned it as a formidable player in the sector.

Amazon announced the launch of its AWS European Sovereign Cloud, an independent cloud for Europe supported by a planned €7.8 billion investment. Separately, TD Cowen raised its price target on the company to $315, citing positive indicators for its advertising business.

AT&T is another name highlighted by JPMorgan. The telecommunications giant has refocused on its core business following recent divestitures, with analysts noting its stable cash flow and dividend potential as attractive features for income-focused investors.

AT&T has completed the deployment of mid-band spectrum acquired from EchoStar across thousands of U.S. cell sites, a move intended to increase 5G download speeds for its mobile and internet customers.

Digital Reality Trust

Digital Realty Trust is also featured on the bank’s focus list. The data center REIT benefits from increasing digital transformation trends and growing demand for cloud infrastructure, providing essential real estate for telecom and technology operations.

In a recent development, Digital Realty Trust entered a joint venture to invest $11 billion in AI data capacity in India. The company also received an upgrade to Buy from HSBC, which raised its price target to $193.

Sirius XM is included among JPMorgan’s preferred media and telecom stocks. The satellite radio and audio entertainment company maintains a unique position in the automotive and subscription audio market, with analysts highlighting its steady subscriber base.

Sirius XM reported third-quarter earnings that surpassed analyst forecasts, with an earnings per share of $0.84 on revenue of $2.16 billion.

T-Mobile is another pick on JPMorgan’s list. The wireless carrier continues to gain market share following its merger with Sprint, with its 5G network expansion driving customer acquisition and revenue growth.

T-Mobile’s board of directors authorized a new shareholder return program of up to $14.6 billion, which is set to run through the end of 2026 and will include stock repurchases and dividends.

Take-Two Interactive Software is also among JPMorgan’s highlighted names. The video game publisher behind franchises like Grand Theft Auto and NBA 2K represents the gaming segment of the media landscape, with analysts noting its strong intellectual property portfolio.

Wells Fargo raised its price target on Take-Two Interactive Software to $288 from $277, maintaining an Overweight rating on the video game publisher.

JPMorgan’s monthly Analyst Focus List serves as a curated selection of investment ideas across various strategies, though the bank notes these recommendations may differ from their fundamental ratings on the stocks.
