title: Beamr outlines 2026 strategy focused on autonomous vehicle market
content:
Beamr Imaging Ltd. (BMR) issued a shareholder letter outlining its 2025 achievements and 2026 strategic direction, with plans to convert validated use cases into commercial agreements across autonomous vehicle, artificial intelligence and media markets.

The video optimization technology company reported entering the autonomous vehicle market in 2025, transitioning from validation to commercialization planning. CEO Sharon Carmel stated the company completed proof-of-concept evaluations with automotive suppliers and AV developers, validating up to 50% reduction in video storage while maintaining model stability with accuracy changes below 2%.

Key 2025 milestones included launching a GPU-accelerated video compression solution for autonomous vehicles at NVIDIA GTC Paris in June. The company received the NAB Show Product of the Year award in April for its AV1 codec solution and won Best of Show at IBC 2025 for a live 4K sports broadcasting demonstration.

Beamr joined Amazon Web Services’ ISV Accelerate program in February 2025 and completed SOC 2 Type II compliance certification in December. The company presented at six industry shows across media, entertainment and autonomous vehicle sectors.

For 2026, Beamr plans to focus on execution through partner-led deployments and production-ready solutions. The company aims to establish relationships with what it terms "lighthouse" accounts - global industry leaders requiring extended evaluation cycles due to video’s role in their infrastructure.

Beamr’s technology reduces video file sizes while preserving quality, targeting applications in media, user-generated content, machine learning and autonomous vehicles. The company holds 53 patents and previously won an Emmy Award for Technology and Engineering.

title: Evercore ISI reaffirms Amazon stock rating, sees AI assistant Rufus boosting sales
content:
Investing.com - Evercore ISI has reiterated an Outperform rating and $335.00 price target on Amazon.com (NASDAQ:AMZN), citing potential growth from the company’s AI-powered shopping assistant Rufus. This target represents approximately 48% upside from the current price of $226.50, aligning with the broader analyst consensus which remains strongly bullish on the stock with a 1.33 recommendation rating (where 1 is Strong Buy).

Evercore ISI noted that customers using Rufus during a shopping journey were 60% more likely to complete a purchase, demonstrating significant conversion improvement. This adoption comes before full awareness of Rufus has been reached and before complete rollout of additional AI features like AutoBuy and Predicting Bundling.

The firm also countered market concerns about AI’s impact on advertising, projecting that Rufus could actually increase Amazon’s ad revenue by approximately $4 billion or 3.0% by 2028. As AI shopping helps guide and extract greater intent, the resulting leads become more valuable to marketers.

In total, Evercore ISI estimates that AI-powered commerce could boost Amazon’s 2028 total revenue by up to $31 billion or 3% and increase its 2028 total operating income by up to $6.8 billion or 4.6%.

In other recent news, Amazon’s self-driving unit, Zoox, is recalling 332 vehicles in the United States due to a software error in its Automated Driving Systems, which could pose road safety risks. The U.S. National Highway Traffic Safety Administration highlighted that the software issue may cause vehicles to cross or stop in front of oncoming traffic, increasing crash risks. Additionally, Amazon received a boost from Jefferies, which raised its stock price target from $275 to $300, maintaining a Buy rating. The firm cited potential growth in Amazon Web Services and advancements in artificial intelligence as key factors.

In related developments, Blue Origin, founded by Amazon’s Jeff Bezos, appointed former United Launch Alliance CEO Tory Bruno as president of its National Security Group. Bruno will oversee national security initiatives in his new role. Meanwhile, Target, Whole Foods, and Walmart are set to be added to lawsuits against ByHeart over potentially contaminated baby formula. Foodborne-illness attorney Bill Marler plans to name these retailers as defendants. Lastly, Erste Group issued a positive outlook for U.S. equities, projecting earnings growth for U.S. companies to accelerate in the coming years.

title: Amazon stock price target raised to $300 from $275 at Jefferies
content:
Investing.com - Jefferies has raised its price target on Amazon.com (NASDAQ:AMZN) to $300.00 from $275.00 while maintaining a Buy rating on the stock. This target represents a significant upside from the current price of $226.50, aligning with the broader analyst consensus which remains strongly bullish with a 1.33 recommendation rating (where 1 is Strong Buy).

The firm cited potential for negative sentiment to reverse based on accelerating Amazon Web Services (AWS) growth and improving artificial intelligence capabilities. Jefferies noted that after Amazon’s relative underperformance in 2025, with the stock up only 5% compared to the S&P 500’s 16% gain, the valuation appears attractive at approximately 12x 2026 EV/EBITDA.

AWS sales accelerated from 17% growth in the first half of 2025 to 20% in the third quarter, and Jefferies sees potential for further acceleration to mid-20s growth in 2026, with margins expected to remain in the healthy mid-30s range.

While many investors perceive Amazon to be behind peers like Microsoft and Google in AI development, Jefferies believes Amazon’s market-leading position in cloud computing and the eventual shift in AI from model training to running AI applications in production position AWS to become a key provider of AI development and inferencing solutions for enterprises.

In other recent news, Amazon’s self-driving unit, Zoox, is recalling 332 vehicles in the United States due to a software error in its Automated Driving Systems. This issue, announced by the U.S. National Highway Traffic Safety Administration, could lead to dangerous road situations by causing vehicles to cross or stop in front of oncoming traffic, increasing the risk of crashes. Additionally, Target, Whole Foods, and Walmart are set to be added as defendants in lawsuits against ByHeart, a baby formula manufacturer, over formula potentially contaminated with botulism-causing spores. Meanwhile, Blue Origin, founded by Amazon’s Jeff Bezos, has appointed former United Launch Alliance CEO Tory Bruno as president of its newly formed National Security Group. Bruno will oversee the company’s national security initiatives in his new role. The U.S. Department of Energy has also announced the Genesis Mission, partnering with 24 organizations to use artificial intelligence to enhance scientific discovery and national security. This initiative aligns with President Trump’s AI Action Plan aimed at bolstering America’s scientific capabilities. Lastly, Erste Group has provided a positive outlook for U.S. equities, predicting an acceleration in earnings growth for U.S. companies to 15% year-over-year in 2026.

title: Hedge fund portfolios: Here’s the latest look at where these investors stood
content:
Investing.com -- Hedge funds headed into the final quarter of the year with a markedly more cautious stance, trimming long exposure sharply to 169% from 277% a month earlier, the lowest level since December 2024, while the short book stood at -69%.

"Heading into October, Hedge Funds decreased their risk profile after remaining elevated throughout the summer," Jefferies strategist Steven DeSanctis said in a note.

The reduction was driven largely by cuts to Secular Growth, DeSanctis said, citing data as of September 30. Its portfolio weight dropped to 49.3% from 60.3%, moving below its long-term average.

Healthcare, despite recent strong performance and renewed investor interest, saw the biggest reduction, with its weight falling by more than 614 basis points (bps) to 16.2%.

Technology, by contrast, remained the largest sector exposure at around 31.6%, up 50 bps.

Within concentrated positions, exposure to the so-called Sweet 16 group eased from a record 51.4% to 46.7%, though it remained significantly overweight versus the S&P 500. Large reductions were seen in Microsoft, Meta Platforms, and Amazon, even as those three names still accounted for more than one-fifth of total portfolio weight.

"The trimming down of the Sweet 16 portfolio essentially occurred between MSFT, loss of 4.7%, Meta by 3.5%, and AMZN by 3.4%, but these 3 stocks were still close to 23% of the total weight," DeSanctis noted.

At the sector level, cyclicals picked up weight, rising to 48.5%, the highest since May, although still underweight relative to the benchmark. Materials and Energy both moved from net short to net long position, while Industrials saw a modest increase to 6.7% from 5.7%.

At the same time, exposure to Financials and Consumer Discretionary was reduced. Another notable shift was in bond proxies, which moved to a net long position for the first time since June, reaching 2.2%, signaling a more defensive tilt.

Stocks that moved from short to long positions outperformed in November, the report showed, rising 5% during the month and more than 21% year to date, ahead of the broader market.

Meanwhile, the Popular Shorts group fell sharply over the same period.

title: BofA unveils its top 10 U.S. ideas for Q1 2026
content:
Investing.com -- Bank of America has released its new list of high-conviction U.S. stock ideas for the first quarter of 2026, featuring nine Buy-rated names and one Underperform.

The quarterly lineup highlights companies that the bank believes have “significant market and business-related catalysts in the quarter ahead,” according to BofA strategist Anthony Cassamassino.

The Buy recommendations span nine industries and include Amazon, Boeing, Cigna, Constellation Energy, Dollar General, Equinix, Merck, Spotify, and Vertex Pharmaceuticals.

The lone Underperform is homebuilder Lennar. BofA said the list is designed as a short-term set of opportunities and will be updated only at the start of each quarter unless ratings change.

While artificial intelligence remains a key theme, the bank emphasised that “the drivers for the broader list are more diverse.” BofA noted that it sees legislative developments as a potential catalyst for Cigna, while for Merck, the bank highlighted its “attractive valuation.”

Dollar General, meanwhile, could benefit from “higher-than-expected tax refunds in F1Q26.”

Amazon is the firm’s top large-cap internet stock, “given exposure to AI vs AWS,” and the bank’s “expectation for AWS revenue growth to accelerate into 2026.”

“For Boeing, we expect 1Q will focus on commercial production rates,” added BofA. “Production remaining stable is a key to investor confidence and the name building momentum for the year.”

For the wider market, BofA’s U.S. equity strategist Savita Subramanian warned that “there is no way to sugar coat it – the S&P 500 is expensive.”

Still, she pointed to Health Care, IT and Real Estate as sectors that “screen attractive near-term.”

title: Bank of America unveils top 10 stock picks for Q1 2026 with diverse catalysts
content:
Investing.com - Bank of America released its top 10 stock recommendations for the first quarter of 2026, featuring nine Buy ratings and one Underperform across nine different industries.

The firm’s list of Buy-rated stocks includes Amazon.com (NASDAQ:AMZN), Boeing (NYSE:BA), Cigna (NYSE:CI), Constellation Energy (NASDAQ:CEG), Dollar General (NYSE:DG), Equinix (NASDAQ:EQIX), Merck (NYSE:MRK), Spotify (NYSE:SPOT), and Vertex Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:VRTX). Lennar Corp (NYSE:LEN) received the sole Underperform rating.

For the broader market, Bank of America’s U.S. Equity Strategist Savita Subramanian states that "there is no way to sugar coat it—the S&P 500 is expensive."

Despite overall market concerns, the firm suggests there are still opportunities, noting that Health Care, IT, and Real Estate sectors "screen attractive near-term."

In other recent news, Dollar General has been the focus of multiple analyst updates following its third-quarter earnings report. The company reported sales of $10.65 billion, aligning with analyst expectations and marking a 2.5% growth in comparable sales. Earnings per share reached $1.28, significantly surpassing Truist Securities’ estimate of $0.96. UBS responded to these strong results by raising its price target to $143.00, highlighting traffic growth and margin recovery. Guggenheim also increased its price target to $140.00, citing a significant earnings beat driven by gross margin improvements. JPMorgan upgraded Dollar General to Overweight, with a price target of $166.00, based on anticipated same-store sales growth supported by store expansion and renovation initiatives. Truist Securities maintained a Hold rating but increased its price target to $129.00, acknowledging the company’s operational simplifications. These developments reflect the analysts’ positive outlook on Dollar General’s ongoing strategies and financial performance.

title: AI spending, strong corporate profits, Fed rate cuts seen as key to 2026 US stock market
content:
By Lewis Krauskopf

NEW YORK, Dec 31 (Reuters) - The U.S. stock market is closing the books on a third straight year of double-digit percentage gains. A fourth stellar year in 2026 may be a tall order, requiring strong earnings, a dovish Federal Reserve and strong artificial intelligence spending.

The bull market for U.S. stocks that started in October 2022 has been propelled by AI optimism, interest rate cuts and the economy continuing to grow despite fears of a recession. The gains were achieved during a rollercoaster year which saw shares dive after the Trump administration unveiled bigger-than-expected tariffs in April. The benchmark S&P 500 climbed more than 16% in 2025, after rising 23% in 2024 and 24% in 2023.

For another year of strong double-digit percentage returns, markets need "everything firing on all cylinders," said Sam Stovall, chief investment strategist at CFRA.

"A lot of headwinds indicate to me that while we could end up with a surprisingly good year, I don’t think it will be another great year," said Stovall, who has a year-end 2026 price target of 7,400, which would be up 8% from current levels.

Many market strategists foresee a strong 2026, with some S&P 500 targets equating to over 10% gains, including Deutsche Bank’s target of 8,000, which is about 17% higher for the index.

WILL EARNINGS AND AI PROVIDE A LIFT?

Stock bulls point to the upbeat outlook for U.S. corporate profits. Earnings among S&P 500 companies are projected up over 15% in 2026, on the back of a solid 13% rise in 2025, according to Tajinder Dhillon, head of earnings research at LSEG.

Earnings growth is expected to be driven by a broader swath of companies, as fiscal stimulus and easier monetary policy help shore up the economy and consumer spending, rather than confined to a small group of tech and tech-related behemoths. Those megacap companies, which include Nvidia, Apple and Amazon and are known as the "Magnificent Seven", posted 37% profit growth in 2024 compared to 7% for the rest of the S&P 500, according to LSEG’s Dhillon.

In 2026, that gap is poised to narrow significantly: the Mag 7 are expected to have earnings growth of 23% against 13% for the rest of the index.

"An improvement in earnings growth for many of the 493 other stocks in the S&P 500 -- and we’ve seen some of that already -- that certainly would help the stock market get to double-digit returns next year," said Kristina Hooper, chief market strategist at Man Group.

Profit growth will be critical because stock valuations will be hard-pressed to expand beyond lofty levels, investors said.

One boost to valuations has been excitement over AI, including massive spending for infrastructure and expected voracious demand for its application. Recently, questions about the returns from the capital spending dented tech and other AI-linked shares, and will likely remain a critical theme in 2026.

"If companies start to pull back on the capex that they have already guided to and the market loses confidence in the returns that the AI investment will generate...you’re probably looking at more of a flat or even a modestly down year," said Jeff Buchbinder, chief equity strategist for LPL Financial.

DOVISH FED, MIXED HISTORICAL SIGNALS AND WILDCARDS

Another critical factor for a strong stock market year, investors said, is the economy softening enough to pave the way for calming inflation and more rate cuts, but not so much that it falls into a recession. Fed funds futures indicate investors expect at least two more quarter-point reductions in 2026 following 175 basis points of cuts in 2024 and 2025.

"Probably the biggest driver I’d be looking for is the Fed maintaining a dovish stance," said Yung-Yu Ma, chief investment strategist at PNC Financial Services Group.

Investors are watching President Donald Trump’s choice of a Fed chair, expected early in 2026, as a sign the central bank will be more dovish but are concerned about its independence being tested.

Historical data yields a mixed story about potential returns in 2026. On the plus side, of seven bull markets that made it to year four since 1950, the fourth year has averaged a gain of 12.8%, posting positive performance for the year in six of seven occasions, according to LPL Research.

However, U.S. midterm election years, when the election of a new Congress injects uncertainty about the makeup of the federal government, tend to be subpar. The S&P 500 is up just 3.8% on average in midterm years, compared to an average 11% during the other three years of a presidential term, according to CFRA’s Stovall.

There are also a host of possible wildcards. For example, while tariffs receded as the dominant market issue after driving extreme volatility in early 2025, the relationship between the U.S. and China, the world’s two largest economies, could sway stocks in 2026, said PNC’s Ma.

"There’s actually a possibility for a breakthrough between the U.S. and China that could be a positive catalyst that is not baked into expectations," he said.

title: Bezos, Huang, Dell, Zuckerberg - who cashed out the most in 2025?
content:
Investing.com -- Billionaire founders, CEOs, and directors of major tech companies sold more than $16 billion worth of shares in 2025, with Amazon’s Jeff Bezos leading the pack.

Bezos topped the list by selling 25 million Amazon shares worth $5.7 billion, according to data from Washington Service tracking insider sales through December 30. The Amazon chair executed these sales under a pre-arranged 10b5-1 trading plan that coincided with his wedding to Lauren Sanchez. This brings his total stock sales since 2002 to over $50 billion.

Former Oracle CEO Safra Catz ranked second, selling 12.5 million shares valued at $2.5 billion. Catz, who stepped down as CEO in September to become vice chair of the board, exercised and sold performance-based stock options received earlier in the year.

Michael Dell of Dell Technologies sold over 16.2 million shares worth $2.2 billion. Unlike most other executives on the list, Dell did not use a 10b5-1 plan for his transactions, which occurred in June and October. Dell and his wife recently announced a $6.25 billion donation to launch "Trump Accounts" for 25 million American children.

Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang sold 6 million shares for over $1 billion as the company became the world’s first $4 trillion company in July and first $5 trillion company in October.

Arista Networks CEO Jayshree Ullal sold 7.56 million shares worth $975.8 million. Despite these sales, most of her $6.1 billion net worth remains tied to her 2.4% stake in the company.

Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg and his associated charitable initiatives sold 1.38 million shares worth $944.8 million. Zuckerberg has previously stated his intention to donate 99% of his Meta shares during his lifetime.

The sales coincided with mega-cap tech companies leading a disproportionate share of U.S. stock market gains in 2025. Nearly all the top sellers used 10b5-1 trading plans, which signal selling intentions in advance.

title: US draws bulk of state-owned investment in 2025 as assets hit record $60 trln
content:
By Libby George

LONDON, Jan 1 (Reuters) - Sovereign wealth and public pension fund investors poured a whopping $132 billion - roughly half of their investments last year - into the United States in 2025, while big emerging markets drew in almost a third less than in 2024, an annual report showed on Thursday.

These huge investors together with central banks notched a record $60 trillion in assets under management last year, the report from Global SWF showed, with sovereign wealth funds accounting for two-thirds of the money invested in the U.S. during the year.

"There was a change in paradigm when it comes to recipient countries," Global SWF managing director Diego Lopez wrote in the report, adding that the world’s largest economy benefited from spending focused on digital infrastructure, data centres and AI companies.

Sovereign wealth fund assets alone hit a fresh record - $15 trillion - according to the report, which uses a combination of public data and official reports to monitor the assets and spending of the world’s state-owned investors, including wealth and pension funds and central banks.

Overall, sovereign wealth fund investments grew by 35% to $179.3 billion.

EMERGING MARKET SLIDE

Funneling investments to the U.S. came at the expense of emerging markets, however - despite their outsized performance in 2025.

"The big losers were emerging markets, especially China, India, Indonesia, and Saudi Arabia, which received in 2025 disappointing levels of investment: a 28% drop from 2024, and only 15% of the total," Lopez wrote.

Private credit investors by contrast have begun a pivot to emerging markets, seeking stronger returns and more advantageous project structures, the report showed.

All 11 new sovereign funds launched during the year originated in emerging markets, but with crude oil prices under pressure, 2026 could bring change for the current big spenders. Saudi Arabia already has plans to refocus spending amid low oil prices and delays to flagship projects.

"New capital will depend on the source of revenue: SWFs reliant on oil will find 2026 another tough year as revenues stagnate, while natural gas and metals such as copper spur new flows," Lopez wrote.

US RETAINS ITS PULLING POWER

The investment flow figures, Lopez noted, do not include the estimated $2.2 trillion of Magnificent 7 stocks - Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Nvidia, Meta and Tesla - that sovereign wealth and pension funds already hold.

The shift to the U.S. underscores its pulling power, even as investors sought to diversify as President Donald Trump reshapes the economy.

It also comes after eye-watering pledges from Gulf countries to invest billions in the United States - often through their powerful sovereign wealth funds.

Saudi Arabia was Trump’s first foreign visit during his second term, and he hosted influential Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman at the White House in November. Trump said Saudi Arabia had agreed to invest $600 billion in the U.S., while bin Salman pledged to increase the total to $1 trillion.

Abu Dhabi has pledged $1.4 trillion for U.S. investments, while Qatar plans $500 billion over the next decade.

Saudi Arabia’s Public Investment Fund (PIF) with $36.2 billion committed, of which 80% will go to its takeover of games maker Electronic Arts, and Abu Dhabi’s Mubadala, with a record $32.7 billion, were the top two spenders.

Gulf funds PIF, Abu Dhabi-based L’imad Holding Company PJSC, Qatar Investment Authority are also key financial backers of Paramount Skydance’s hostile bid to take over Warner Bros Discovery.

Canada’s CPP and La Caisse as well as Singapore’s GIC joined PIF and Mubadala in the top five spenders.

title: Citizens reiterates Market Outperform rating on Roku stock, citing catalysts
content:
The firm highlighted Roku’s significant market penetration, noting the platform has reached over 50% of broadband households and captured 46% of U.S. streaming hours in August, according to Comscore data. This market dominance has helped drive Roku’s impressive 16.6% revenue growth over the last twelve months.

Citizens identified multiple catalysts for Roku heading into 2026, including expanding third-party DSP partnerships with companies like Amazon, continued premium subscriber growth, and a new Home Screen rollout planned for the company.

Additional growth drivers mentioned include continued engagement increases on The Roku Channel and expanding international monetization opportunities for the streaming platform provider.

The research firm expects Roku to maintain cost discipline while pursuing these growth initiatives, which it projects will result in meaningful free cash flow growth over the next several years.

In other recent news, Roku Inc. announced an expanded multi-year partnership with Nielsen to integrate Roku’s data into Nielsen’s advanced campaign measurement solutions. This collaboration aims to provide advertisers with more accurate performance results, leveraging Roku’s significant share of over 21% of all TV viewing. Additionally, Morgan Stanley upgraded Roku’s stock from Equalweight to Overweight, citing optimistic projections for the company’s platform revenue growth in 2026 and beyond. The investment bank raised its price target to $135.00, reflecting an increased estimate of platform revenue growth to 19%. Similarly, Jefferies upgraded Roku from Hold to Buy, also setting a price target of $135.00, and indicated potential for platform revenue growth to exceed Wall Street’s expectations. Citizens maintained its Market Outperform rating for Roku, emphasizing the company’s dominant position in the U.S. streaming market. The firm noted that Roku has reached over 50% of broadband households and captured 46% of U.S. streaming hours as of August. These developments highlight Roku’s strategic moves and favorable analyst outlooks.

This article was generated with the support of AI and reviewed by an editor. For more information see our T&C.
